ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for fried chicken gyoza; 'dumpling food product' (FOODON_03302648) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers gyoza-type dumplings as a category; no specific fried chicken gyoza with mayonnaise entry exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for fried chicken gyoza; left null.

serving.natural_language_portion: One small serving of fried chicken gyoza with mayonnaise typically consists of 5 to 6 pieces weighing approximately 120 grams, as served at a Japanese gyoza specialty restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Gyoza, chicken, fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; adapted with mayonnaise contribution
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for fried chicken gyoza with Japanese mayonnaise exists in primary regional sources (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD). Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for fried chicken dumplings (gyoza) combined with a proportional contribution from Japanese-style mayonnaise (~10 g per 100 g of finished product). Energy, macros, sodium, and fat values reflect the fried preparation and mayonnaise inclusion. All values are per 100 g of the combined finished product.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; dumpling / filled pasta analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried chicken gyoza with mayonnaise. GI estimated at 48 based on the closest analogous food category: filled wheat-flour dumplings (wonton/dumpling type, GI ~45–52) from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The wheat-flour wrapper is the primary glycemic driver; chicken filling and mayonnaise have negligible glycemic contribution. GI 48 is within the low category (≤55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 26.4) / 100 ≈ 12.7, using 120 g serving size and 22.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~576 mg per small serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of fried chicken gyoza with mayonnaise due to its high sodium content (~576 mg per small serving), which represents approximately 25% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; portion control and avoidance of additional soy-based dipping sauces are strongly advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~12.7 per small serving) and refined wheat-flour wrapper
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can consume fried chicken gyoza in moderation; the low GI (48) of the wheat-flour wrapper limits rapid glucose excursion, but the moderate glycemic load per serving (~12.7) and the calorie-dense mayonnaise topping warrant portion control and pairing with non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated total fat and sodium from deep-frying and mayonnaise
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume fried chicken gyoza with mayonnaise sparingly, as the deep-frying process and mayonnaise contribute to elevated total fat (~13.8 g/100 g) and saturated fat (~2.9 g/100 g), and the high sodium content (~480 mg/100 g) may adversely affect blood pressure and endothelial function; grilled or steamed gyoza variants are preferable alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Chicken Gyoza with Mayonnaise (Small) is a Japanese-style appetiser or snack consisting of thin wheat-flour dumpling wrappers filled with seasoned minced chicken, cabbage, garlic, ginger, and sesame oil, then deep-fried or pan-fried until the exterior is golden and crispy. The dish is served with a side of Japanese-style mayonnaise (Kewpie-style), which adds a rich, creamy, and slightly tangy flavour profile to the savoury, juicy filling. The small serving size typically comprises 5 to 6 pieces (~120 g). Gyoza originated in China (as jiaozi) and was adapted in Japan during the post-World War II period, becoming a staple of Japanese izakaya and casual dining culture. In Singapore, fried chicken gyoza is available at Japanese restaurant chains and specialty gyoza outlets such as GyoGyo. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 248 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (~10.5 g), significant fat from frying and mayonnaise (~13.8 g), and carbohydrate from the wheat wrapper (~22 g). Sodium content is notably high (~480 mg/100 g), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 48 (low), making it relatively suitable for blood glucose management when consumed in controlled portions. Allergens include wheat, egg, soy, and sesame.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fried chicken gyoza?
Is fried gyoza high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for fried chicken gyoza with Japanese mayonnaise was found in primary regional sources (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for fried chicken dumplings with a proportional mayonnaise contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed international GI tables. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central fried chicken dumpling data combined with a proportional Japanese mayonnaise contribution (~10 g per 100 g finished product); no exact match exists in primary regional databases. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source data for this food category. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source data for this food category. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g, reflecting trace amounts from frying oil; this is a low but non-zero value consistent with commercial frying. 5. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate based on filled wheat-flour dumplings from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 6. The halal status is marked false as Japanese mayonnaise (Kewpie) typically contains rice vinegar and egg but the restaurant's halal certification status is unconfirmed. 7. The food_name has been normalised from the menu-style label '708D Fried Chicken Gyoza with Mayo Fry S' to the canonical English form 'Fried Chicken Gyoza with Mayonnaise (Small)'; the original menu name is preserved in aliases.
